mnemonics.title: Gradient Points Upward
mnemonics.content: GRADIENT: Gradient Rises At Decreasing rate Indicates Elevation iN Direction of maximal iNcrease. Think: 'GUp' = Gradient Points Up (steepest ascent)

mnemonics.title: Jacobian Matrix Layout
mnemonics.content: JACOBIAN: Just Another Collection Of Basic Indexed Elements, Note matrix arrangement: Outputs × Inputs. Remember: m rows (outputs), n columns (inputs)

mnemonics.title: Hessian Pattern
mnemonics.content: HESSIAN: Holds Every Second derivative, Shows curvature, Indicates Approximation, aNalysis. Think: H is for 'Hill' (positive = valley, negative = peak)

mnemonics.title: Critical Point Check
mnemonics.content: CPR: Critical Point = all Partial derivatives are teRmos (zero). Think: 'Cry PLease Reduce' - at critical point, gradient is zero

acronyms.expansion: Just A Collection Of Basic partials (Jacobian matrix)
acronyms.usage: Remember Jacobian = matrix of all first partials for vector functions

acronyms.expansion: Divergence: Integral of Vector field External (∇·F = sum of partials)
acronyms.usage: For computing divergence of vector fields

acronyms.expansion: Gradient: Rises At Direction (∇f points direction of steepest ascent)

visualTips.visualization: Draw arrows at each point in 2D plane showing direction of steepest ascent - arrows point 'uphill'. At critical points, arrows have zero length (no arrow).
Think of it as m×n grid: m rows for each output function, n columns for each input variable. Fill each cell with corresponding partial derivative ∂fᵢ/∂xⱼ.
Imagine a contour plot: directional derivative = slope you experience when walking in that specific direction. Gradient is the steepest path up the hill.
At critical points, contour lines have 'flat' spots (horizontal tangent). No uphill or downhill direction exists - you're on a plateau, peak, or valley floor.

analogies.analogy: The gradient is like a compass that always points to the steepest uphill direction. In hiking, if you want to reach the summit fastest, follow the gradient. To descend fastest, follow negative gradient.
The Jacobian is like an inspector that checks how a rubber sheet (domain) stretches when transformed (codomain). The determinant tells the area scaling factor.
Hessian is like a speedometer for curvature. Positive = cup (valley), Negative = cap (peak), Zero = flat (saddle or plateau).
At a critical point, all 'traffic' (gradient) stops - the derivative in every direction is zero. It's a stop sign telling you to investigate further to determine if it's a min, max, or saddle.
